1: Psalm 143 is one of the seven classical Penitential Psalms designated in the liturgical tradition of the Western Church (Pss 6, 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, 143). The opening petition appeals to divine faithfulness (ʾĕmûnāh) and righteousness (ṣĕdāqāh) as the grounds for divine answer — not the speaker's merit. "In your faithfulness and righteousness, answer me" — the remarkable request that YHWH's own character attributes (ʾĕmûnāh and ṣedeq) become the grounds of rescue; the same attributes that could condemn the wicked are here appealed to for salvation of the penitent. This captures the paradox of grace: divine righteousness that punishes sin also delivers those who acknowledge their guilt.
2: "Do not bring your servant into judgment, for no one alive can stand as righteous before you" — kî lōʾ yiṣdaq lĕpānêkā kol-ḥāy (for no living person is righteous before you). This is the starkest universal-guilt statement in the Psalter — more sweeping than Ps 14:1–3 ("there is no one who does good, not even one"), since it applies to "any living person" without exception. Paul cites this verse in Romans 3:20 and Galatians 2:16 as the foundation for his argument that justification is not by works of the law: "By works of the law no one will be justified." The verse becomes for Paul the OT's own recognition that the covenant's demands exceed any human's capacity to meet them.
3: "For the enemy has hunted me down and crushed my life to the ground; he has forced me to dwell in darkness like those who died long ago" — yōšib bĕmaḥăšakkîm kĕmētê ʿôlām (he has forced me to dwell in darkness like those long dead). "Forced me to dwell in darkness like those long dead" — the imagery of being thrust into Sheol's conditions while still alive: the long dead (mētê ʿôlām) are those whose names have been forgotten, who no longer inhabit the community of the living. Being made to "dwell in darkness" while alive echoes Ps 88 — the condition of being in Sheol before biological death, the living experience of covenant abandonment.
4: "So my spirit grows faint within me; my heart within me is filled with dismay" — rûḥî ʿālay tittʿaṭṭēp (my spirit within me grows faint) — the same verb as Ps 142:3. "My heart within me is filled with dismay (bĕtôkî yiššômēm libbî)" — šāmēm (desolate, appalled, stunned) is the word used for the desolation of the destroyed temple (Lam 1:4, 13, 16) and of ravaged land (Jer 12:11; Isa 54:1). The heart is desolated as completely as a bombed landscape. The Babylonian exile background of these psalms is palpable: the language of individual distress borrows the vocabulary of national catastrophe.
5: "I call to mind the days of old; I meditate on all you have done; I reflect on the work of your hands" — zākartî yāmîm miqqedem hāgîtî bĕkol-paʿālĕkā. The practice of memorial meditation — deliberately calling to mind the covenant acts of the past — is one of the Psalter's primary resources for faith under pressure. Psalms 77:11–12 uses the same practice: "I will remember the deeds of the LORD; yes, I will remember your wonders of old. I will meditate on all your work and muse on your mighty deeds." The Passover seder makes this a communal liturgical act: "In every generation, each person is obligated to see themselves as if they personally left Egypt." The past becomes present through intentional memory.
6: "I reach out my hands toward you; my whole being longs for you like a land parched for rain" — the two postures of prayer: reaching hands toward YHWH (the standard upward-extended-hands prayer gesture; Pss 28:2; 63:4; Lam 1:17; 2:19) and the thirst-image for longing (Ps 42:1–2: "As a deer pants for streams of water, so my soul pants for you, O God"; Ps 63:1: "my soul thirsts for you, my flesh longs for you, as in a dry and weary land where there is no water"; Isa 44:3; 55:1). The parched land that longs for rain is the desert landscape of Israel — the absolute dependency on rainfall as a divine gift makes the rain-longing a powerful image of total dependence. Selah.
7: "Answer me quickly, O LORD — my spirit is failing! Do not hide your face from me, or I will be like those who sink into the grave" — the urgency adverb "quickly (māhēr)" reflects the medical-emergency character of the prayer: delay is lethal. "Do not hide your face (al-tastēr pānêkā)" — the hidden divine face is the most severe form of divine silence in the Psalter (Pss 13:1; 22:24; 27:9; 44:24; 88:14; 89:46; Isa 59:2; 64:7; Ezek 39:23–24). "Like those who sink into the grave (kĕyōrĕdê bôr)" — the same pit-descent formula as Ps 88:4; 143:3. Divine absence and death are causally linked: the hidden face produces the descent into the pit.
8: "Let me hear of your steadfast love in the morning, for in you I put my trust; show me the path I should take, for I lift my soul to you" — "in the morning (babbōqer)" — the morning as the time of divine answer is a recurring motif in the Psalter: the worshipper kept a night vigil waiting for the morning oracle of divine favor and deliverance (Pss 5:3; 46:5; 90:14; 130:5–6; Lam 3:22–23). The morning after the night of suffering is the expected moment of divine ḥesed announcement. "Show me the path (hôdîʿênî derekî-zû ʾēlēk bāh)" — the path-metaphor unites guidance, wisdom, and covenant: to be shown the right path by YHWH is to receive both practical direction and moral formation.
9: "Rescue me from my enemies, O LORD — I run to you for shelter" — ʾêlêkā kissîtî (to you I have hidden myself, I have taken shelter). The verb kāsāh means to cover, hide, conceal — the speaker has covered themselves with YHWH as one hides under a cloak or in a cave. The divine name/presence itself becomes the hiding place (Ps 27:5: "For in the day of trouble he will conceal me in his shelter; in the secret of his tent he will hide me"; Ps 31:20: "In the shelter of your presence you hide them"). The cave of v. 142 has given way to the divine presence itself as the ultimate shelter.
10: "Teach me to do your will, for you are my God; may your good Spirit lead me on a level path" — rûḥăkā ṭôbāh tanḥēnî bĕʾereṣ mîšôr (your good Spirit will lead me on a level land/plain). "Good Spirit (rûaḥ ṭôbāh)" — one of only three OT occurrences: here, Nehemiah 9:20 ("You gave your good Spirit to instruct them"), and the implied reference in Isaiah 63:10–11. The Spirit as the agent of divine guidance "on a level path" (the opposite of the rough and trap-filled path of vv. 3–4) frames the Spirit's work as guidance into covenant obedience and safety. "Level path (ʾereṣ mîšôr)" — the opposite of the perilous terrain; the plain where walking is safe and clear.
11: "For the sake of your name, O LORD, keep me alive; in your righteousness lead my soul out of its distress" — lĕmaʿan-šĕmĕkā YHWH tĕḥayyênî (for the sake of your name, O LORD, give me life). The appeal to the divine name as the ground of rescue is the highest appeal: YHWH's own reputation, character, and self-disclosure are at stake in how he treats the speaker. Similar appeals appear in Ps 23:3 ("he leads me in paths of righteousness for his name's sake"), Ps 25:11 ("for the sake of your name, O LORD, pardon my guilt"), Ezek 36:22–23, and John 12:28 ("Father, glorify your name"). When the righteous suffer without rescue, the divine name is compromised.
12: "And in your steadfast love silence my enemies and put an end to all who oppress me — for I am your servant" — ûbĕḥasdĕkā tạṣmît ʾōyĕbāy (and in your ḥesed destroy my enemies). The final appeal is to ḥesed — the covenant loyalty that requires the protection of covenant members and the judgment of covenant-breakers. "For I am your servant (kî ʿabdĕkā ʾānî)" — the last word of the psalm is the covenant status that grounds the entire prayer: servant-relationship entitles the speaker to the Master's protection. The Penitential Psalms generally end not with resolution but with petition — the situation remains unresolved, but the speaker's covenant identity remains firm.
